evidence.page_title: Is This a Photograph of a Florida Airport Flooded During Hurricane ...
evidence.title: Is This a Photograph of a Florida Airport Flooded During Hurricane Michael?
evidence.description: As Hurricane Michael weakened into a tropical storm, internet users and reporters shared extraordinary images of the devastation it caused.
evidence.text:
Hurricane Michael made landfall at Mexico Beach, Florida, on 10 October 2018, slamming the Gulf Coast with winds of up to 155 miles per hour and causing widespread property destruction and power outages, as well as two deaths in Florida and Georgia.
As Michael weakened into a tropical storm, internet users and reporters shared extraordinary images of the devastation it caused:
Among those images was one viral photograph which purported to show airplanes almost covered in flood waters at Northwest Florida Beaches International Airport:
The photograph is a fake. It does not show Northwest Florida Beaches International Airport; rather, it was taken from a larger, digitally-edited image created by the artist Nickolay Lamm and used to illustrate the effect that unchecked climate change could have on coastal parts of the United States.
The simulated photograph was used by the website Climate Central in 2013 with the caption: "What LaGuardia [New York] could look like at high tide with 25 feet of sea level rise, an amount that would require a worst-case scenario involving melting of large parts of Greenland and Antarctica":
The same simulation was shared during Hurricane Harvey in August 2017, with social media users falsely reporting that it showed an airport in Houston, Texas:
Houston airport is now the new Houston port! pic.twitter.com/JdOr4coqIb
— isi (@GutierrezIsi) August 28, 2017
On 11 October 2018, officials at Northwest Florida Beaches International Airport announced that commercial flights had been suspended in and out of the airport but that it was "operational," offering further proof that the viral "flooded runway" photograph did not, in fact, show the Panama City airport in the aftermath of Hurricane Michael:

evidence.page_title: Don't share fake photo of flooded airport after Hurricane Harvey
evidence.title: Don't share fake photo of flooded airport after Hurricane Harvey
evidence.description: Someone has taken advantage of the tragedy and shared a doctored photo on Twitter
evidence.text:
Don't share this widespread fake photo of flooded Houston airport after Hurricane Harvey
Someone has taken advantage of the tragedy and shared a doctored photo on Twitter
Hurricane Harvey ravaged Houston, Texas, over the weekend, dumping 30 inches of rain in three days.
Many people lost all of their belongings after the downpours flooded and destroyed their homes.
Pictures and stories of the floods have spread on social media but one photo should not be believed.
Several Twitter accounts, including meteorologists, posted a photo of airplanes on a tarmac almost submerged in floodwater.
A breaking news account shared the image with the caption: "BREAKING: Look at this amazing picture from Houston airport. The flooding planes."
They were quickly alerted to the fact the photo was a fake and warned not to share images like these without checking, The Mirror reported.
The image is actually taken from the Climate Central website, which created a projection of what La Guardia airport in New York would look like at high tide with five feet of sea level rise.
Climate Central claim this could be reality by the year 2100.
But it hasn't just been lifted from the site and shared with incorrect information relating to Houston.
Somebody appears to have digitally manipulated the image further, lifting the water level up to obscure the planes.
The AirLive Twitter account replied to the image, saying: "Be careful, it’s a fake from an older picture in La Guardia."
@CNN @FoxNews Houston airport freezing their customers. This woman is one of the lucky ones. They won't let me get my luggage and come back pic.twitter.com/rvHE5hDVZO— alschuch (@alschuch) August 27, 2017
Other pictures taken inside the airport show that the floor is dry, but passengers are complaining that it is 'freezing' inside the building.
Houston has been devastated by flash floods after Hurricane Harvey brought torrential downpours of 30 inches of rain in three days.
A TV crew spotted one man trapped inside an 18-wheeler truck in rising floodwaters during their live report.
While the camera was still rolling, a reporter alerted police with a rescue boat who successfully saved the man.
image_captions: Do not pass along this false photo(Image: Twitter)

evidence.page_title: This fake picture is being widely shared after Hurricane Harvey
evidence.title: This fake picture is being widely shared after Hurricane Harvey
evidence.description: Pictures and stories of the floods have spread on social media but one photo should not be believe
evidence.text:
This fake flooded Houston airport picture is being widely shared after Hurricane Harvey
Pictures and stories of the floods have spread on social media but one photo should not be believe
Officials fear the death toll from flooding in Texas sparked by Hurricane Harvey could soar once the water recedes from one of America's most sprawling metropolitan centres.
Crews have been overwhelmed by thousands of rescue calls during one of the heaviest downpours in US history and have had little time to search for potential victims.
More than three days after the storm ravaged the Texas coastline as a Category 4 hurricane, authorities had confirmed only three deaths, including a woman killed when heavy rain dislodged a large tree on to her trailer home in the small town of Porter, but unconfirmed reports of others missing or presumed dead are growing.
Pictures and stories of the floods have spread on social media but one photo should not be believed.
Several Twitter accounts, including meteorologists, posted a photo of airplanes on a tarmac almost submerged in floodwater.
A breaking news account shared the image with the caption: "BREAKING: Look at this amazing picture from Houston airport. The flooding planes."
They were quickly alerted to the fact the photo was a fake and warned not to share images like these without checking, The Mirror reported
The image is actually taken from the Climate Central website, which created a projection of what La Guardia airport in New York would look like at high tide with five feet of sea level rise.
Climate Central claim this could be reality by the year 2100.
But it hasn't just been lifted from the site and shared with incorrect information relating to Houston.
Somebody appears to have digitally manipulated the image further, lifting the water level up to obscure the planes.
The AirLive Twitter account replied to the image, saying: "Be careful, it’s a fake from an older picture in La Guardia."

Houston has been devastated by flash floods after Hurricane Harvey brought torrential downpours of 30 inches of rain in three days.
A TV crew spotted one man trapped inside an 18-wheeler truck in rising floodwaters during their live report.

image_captions: Screenshot of video containing AI-generated clips supposedly of Texas floods. Video text says: "Texas - 13.07.2025. No sirens. No alerts. Just silence... then chaos."

A person in military-style clothing stands in the foreground, observing a flooded area where two heavily damaged SUVs are partially submerged and wedged against trees. One vehicle is tilted on its side and a small overturned boat is also caught among the trees. The scene shows the aftermath of a severe flood, with fast-moving water and debris scattered around.
Trees emerge from flood waters along the Guadalupe River in Kerrville, Texas.
A Black man is pictured wearing a Black T-shirt. Overlaid text reads: "Texas Man Arrested After Rescuing Seniors With His Own Boat During Flood."

Two rescue workers in bright yellow and black gear with red helmets navigate floodwaters on an inflatable boat. They are surrounded by tree branches and debris, with a bridge supported by tall concrete columns in the background.
